ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a fried minced prawn-and-fish stick on skewer. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a fried minced prawn-fish composite stick product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific fried prawn-fish snack product. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared snack foods of this type is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Tiger Prawn OnStik piece on a skewer, approximately 40 g, as sold by Old Chang Kee at SGD 2.40 per piece.

nutrient_source.title: Fried prawn / shrimp fritter composite — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15150) and Finfish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15264), adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and batter coating
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Tiger Prawn OnStik exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15150) and cooked fish (NDB 15264) in approximately 60:40 ratio by protein contribution, then applying a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor of approximately 8–10 g fat per 100 g for battered fried seafood, consistent with FAO/INFOODS methodology. Batter carbohydrate contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy wheat flour values. Sodium estimated from typical seasoned fried seafood snack values. All values are medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried battered seafood — analogous estimate based on 'Fish fingers, fried' and 'Battered fried fish' entries; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tiger Prawn OnStik. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous battered fried fish products (GI range 38–52) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The batter coating contributes moderate glycemic carbohydrate; the high protein and fat content from prawn and fish further attenuate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 5.9 g available carbohydrate per 40 g serving) / 100 = 2.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; one piece (40 g) contributes approximately 168 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece occasionally, as the sodium from seasoning and batter accumulates quickly within a 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Batter coating contributes refined carbohydrate with a moderate GI of approximately 45; deep-frying adds significant fat that may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume one piece occasionally as part of a carbohydrate-counted meal, noting that the batter-derived carbohydrate (~5.9 g per piece) and high fat content can contribute to postprandial glucose elevation and should be accounted for in the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes elevated total fat (11.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~115 mg/100 g); however, prawn and fish provide cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the deep-fried fat load and moderate sodium, while recognising that the prawn and fish base provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids; grilled or steamed seafood alternatives are preferred for regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tiger Prawn OnStik is a popular Singaporean fried seafood snack sold by Old Chang Kee, a well-known local snack chain. It consists of minced tiger prawn and fish meat blended together, shaped onto a skewer, coated in a light batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy while the interior is moist, tender, and savoury with a pronounced prawn sweetness and mild fish flavour. It is typically sold as a single piece on a stick for approximately SGD 2.40 and is consumed as a street snack, hawker-style finger food, or light meal accompaniment. Nutritionally, each 40 g piece provides approximately 87 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 4.6 g fat, and 5.9 g carbohydrate. The sodium content of approximately 168 mg per piece warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The batter contributes refined carbohydrate with a moderate glycaemic index of approximately 45, making portion control relevant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The prawn and fish base provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin B12. This snack is classified as a processed, deep-fried seafood product and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or fish allergies. It is widely considered halal. The product is culturally significant as part of Old Chang Kee's iconic Singaporean snack repertoire alongside curry puffs and fishballs.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Tiger Prawn OnStik exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of the accessed date. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp and cooked fish entries with deep-frying oil absorption adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for estimation, and no Singapore-specific data is available for this product. 2. Nutrient values are estimated composites; an exact laboratory analysis of the Old Chang Kee Tiger Prawn OnStik product would be required to achieve high confidence. 3. Halal status is assumed based on Old Chang Kee's general halal certification in Singapore but should be verified against current certification. 4. Serving size of 40 g is an estimate based on typical Old Chang Kee OnStik product weight; actual weight may vary slightly. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product has been published. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects trace levels typical of deep-frying in refined vegetable oils; this is not confirmed zero. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are medium-confidence values.
